perturbation.original_text: Subject to paragraph 3 below, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any and all materials or infor mation not in the public domain that either party receives or acquires from the other party in connection with any activities related thereto, and whether disclosed or made available prior to the date of thisagreement and whether in writing, electronically, orally, visually or otherwise and any notes,summaries or other materials in whatever medium or format (“Notes”) created by theReceiving Party and derived from such information or materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to paragraph 3 below, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean materials or information that either party receives or acquires from the other party in connection with any activities related thereto, disclosed or made available prior to the date of thisagreement and whether in writing. Notes, summaries or other materials created by theReceiving Party and derived from such information or materials are not considered confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting confidential information to only written materials and excluding notes/summaries derived from the original information, the clause fails to protect all forms of proprietary information as required under UK law regarding trade secrets. This omission increases the risk of misappropriation of valuable intellectual property.
perturbation.law_explanation: The amended text contradicts Regulation 2 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, which defines a trade secret as information that is secret, has commercial value because it is secret, and has been subject to reasonable steps to keep it secret. By excluding derivative notes and summaries from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract fails to protect information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the Regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 16 CFR § 2.1 - How initiated. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 16 CFR § 2.1 - How initiated. CFR prev | next § 2.1 How initiated. Commission investigations and inquiries may be originated upon the request of the President, Congress , governmental agencies, or the Attorney General; upon referrals by the courts; upon complaint by members of the public; or by the Commission upon its own initiative. The Commission has delegated to the Director, Deputy Directors, and Assistant Directors of the Bureau of Competition, the Director, Deputy Directors, and Associate Directors of the Bureau of Consumer Protection and, the Regional Directors and Assistant Regional Directors of the Commission 's regional offices, without power of redelegation, limited authority to initiate investigations. The Director of the Bureau of Competition has also been delegated, without power of redelegation, authority to open investigations in response to requests pursuant to an agreement under the International Antitrust Enforcement Assistance Act, 15 U.S.C. 6201 et seq., if the requests do not ask the Commission to use process. Before responding to such a request, the Bureau Director shall transmit the proposed response to the Secretary and the Secretary shall notify the Commission of the proposed response. If no Commissioner objects within three days following the Commission 's receipt of such notification, the Secretary shall inform the Bureau Director that he or she may proceed. [ 48 FR 41374 , Sept. 15, 1983, as amended at 50 FR 53304 , Dec. 31, 1985; 65 FR 67259 , Nov. 9, 2000] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Law Explanation, which states that excluding derivative notes and summaries from the definition of Confidential Information fails to protect information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018. The original contract includes "any notes,summaries or other materials in whatever medium or format ('Notes') created by the Receiving Party and derived from such information or materials" in its definition of Confidential Information. The changed text explicitly states that "Notes, summaries or other materials created by theReceiving Party and derived from such information or materials are not considered confidential". The scraped snippets are not directly relevant here as they are not focused on trade secrets. The Perturbation explanation also supports this, indicating that the omission increases the risk of misappropriation of valuable intellectual property.

perturbation.original_text: A Receiving Party agrees, acknowledges and undertakes to (i) hold the ConfidentialInformation of the Disclosing Party in strictest confidence, and subject to the terms ofthis Agreement, not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party unless it is aregulated investment body, solicitor, accountant or private investors known to the Party.Such information shall be shared freely.
perturbation.changed_text: A Receiving Party agrees, acknowledges and undertakes to (i) hold the ConfidentialInformation of the Disclosing Party in strictest confidence, and subject to the terms of this Agreement, not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception that allows disclosure to certain professional advisors and investors, this significantly restricts the ability of the receiving party to engage in legitimate business activities.  It creates a legal risk because UK law recognises the need for reasonable exceptions to confidentiality for normal business operations and seeking professional advice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied terms of good faith and reasonableness in UK contract law.
perturbation.law_citation: MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company SA v Cottonex Anstalt [2016] EWCA Civ 789
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the implied terms of good faith and reasonableness in UK contract law.  While there isn't a specific statute mandating exceptions for disclosure to professional advisors, the courts imply a duty of good faith and reasonableness in all contracts. Removing the exception for advisors could be seen as an unreasonable restriction on the Receiving Party's ability to conduct business and could be challenged as a breach of this implied duty. The MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company SA v Cottonex Anstalt [2016] EWCA Civ 789 case highlights the courts' approach to interpreting contracts in a commercially reasonable way, considering the practical realities of business.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for disclosure to specific parties (regulated investment body, solicitor, accountant, or private investors), while the changed text removes this exception. This omission, as the Perturbation Explanation notes, significantly restricts the receiving party's ability to engage in legitimate business activities. The Law Explanation further supports this, stating that the removal could be seen as an unreasonable restriction. While neither scraped snippet directly addresses this specific scenario, the removal of the exception contradicts the implied terms of good faith and reasonableness, a principle often applied to contracts to ensure commercial viability.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request, the Receiving Party may provide confirmation that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall certify in writing' to 'may provide confirmation' weakens the obligation of the Receiving Party to provide a written certification of the destruction or return of confidential information. Under UK GDPR laws, especially if personally identifiable information is part of the confidential information, the data controller (Disclosing Party) is required to ensure the data processor (Receiving Party) appropriately handles and destroys the data, including providing proof of deletion. This modification potentially makes the contract non-compliant with GDPR requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Article 28(3)(h) of the GDPR which mandates that the processor, at the choice of the controller, deletes or returns all personal data to the controller after the end of the provision of services relating to processing, and deletes existing copies unless Union or Member State law requires storage of the personal data. A written certification provides evidence of compliance with this requirement. By weakening this requirement to a discretionary 'may provide confirmation', the contract risks non-compliance with GDPR if the confidential information contains personal data.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to 'certify in writing' the return or destruction of confidential information, while the changed text weakens this to 'may provide confirmation'. The Law Explanation states that Article 28(3)(h) of the GDPR mandates that the processor, at the choice of the controller, deletes or returns all personal data. The explanation argues that a written certification provides evidence of compliance, and weakening this to a discretionary 'may provide confirmation' risks non-compliance. This change constitutes a legal contradiction because it removes a concrete obligation to provide written proof of compliance, potentially undermining GDPR requirements, even though a relevant law snippet was not provided.

perturbation.original_text: Each Receiving Party acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement by the Receiving Party may cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party and that the Disclosing Party may have no adequate remedy at law. Accordingly, each party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights and Tabun Kitchen 151 Copse Hill, London, SW20 0SU3 remedies it may have, at law, in equity or otherwise, to seek injunctive relief or similar such orders in any court of competent jurisdiction directing or prohibiting certain acts which may be necessary to protect the Confidential Information and the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Receiving Party acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement by the Receiving Party may cause harm to the Disclosing Party. Accordingly, each party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights and remedies it may have, at law, to seek damages in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgment of 'irreparable harm' and the right to 'seek injunctive relief' limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party. UK law, specifically regarding intellectual property and confidential information, recognises that monetary damages are often insufficient to compensate for the loss caused by a breach of confidence, and injunctive relief is a crucial remedy.  This modification weakens the Disclosing Party's protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the spirit and availability of remedies under Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981, which grants the High Court the power to grant injunctions in all cases in which it appears to the court to be just and convenient to do so. By removing the specific reference to injunctive relief, the agreement could be interpreted as intending to limit the Disclosing Party's ability to seek this important equitable remedy, even in situations where damages are inadequate to address the harm caused by a breach of confidence. While the Act doesn't mandate the inclusion of injunctive relief in contracts, the deletion suggests an attempt to restrict remedies normally available under UK law for breaches of confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the acknowledgment of 'irreparable harm' and the right to 'seek injunctive relief'. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the spirit and availability of remedies under Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981, which grants the High Court the power to grant injunctions. While the snippets don't directly quote Section 37, the first snippet confirms the existence of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and the second snippet provides context, clarifying its role in consolidating laws related to the Supreme Court of England and Wales. The removal suggests an attempt to restrict remedies normally available for breaches of confidentiality, thus a contradiction exists.
